# Walt Disney

American film producer

Top Questions

### What was the cause of Walt Disney’s death?

Walt Disney died in 1966 at the age of 65. The cause of death was [lung cancer](https://www.britannica.com/science/lung-cancer) .

### Does Walt Disney’s family still own Disney?

Walt Disney’s family does not own [Disney](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Walt-Disney) . In 1957 there was an IPO, and it became a public company. In the ensuing years, the number of stocks held by the family decreased. Today the family reportedly holds less than 3 percent of Disney’s stock.

### What were Walt Disney’s final words?

Shortly before he died in 1966, Walt Disney reportedly wrote these words on a note: Ron Miller, 2 Way Down Cellar, Kurt [misspelled as Kirt] Russell, and CIA—Mobley. It is believed that Disney was writing about future projects. Disney was known to be impressed with [Kurt Russell](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Kurt-Russell) , who was then a 15-year-old rising actor. In 1966 Russell appeared in his first [Disney](https://www.britannica.com/money/Disney-Company) movie, _Follow Me, Boys!_

**Walt Disney** (born December 5, 1901, [Chicago](https://www.britannica.com/place/Chicago) , [Illinois](https://www.britannica.com/place/Illinois-state) , U.S.—died December 15, 1966, [Burbank](https://www.britannica.com/place/Burbank-California) , California) was an American [motion-picture](https://www.britannica.com/art/film) and [television](https://www.britannica.com/technology/television-technology) producer and showman, famous as a pioneer of animated cartoon films and as the creator of such cartoon characters as [Mickey Mouse](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Mickey-Mouse) and [Donald Duck](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Donald-Duck) . He also planned and built [Disneyland](https://www.britannica.com/place/Disneyland) , a huge amusement park that opened near [Los Angeles](https://www.britannica.com/place/Los-Angeles-California) in 1955, and before his death he had begun building a second such park, [Walt Disney World](https://www.britannica.com/place/Walt-Disney-World-Resort) , near [Orlando](https://www.britannica.com/place/Orlando-Florida) , Florida. The [Disney Company](https://www.britannica.com/money/Disney-Company) he founded has become one of the world’s largest entertainment conglomerates.

## Early life

Walter Elias Disney was the fourth son of Elias Disney, a [peripatetic](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/peripatetic) carpenter, farmer, and building contractor, and his wife, Flora Call, who had been a public school teacher. When Walt was little more than an infant, the family moved to a farm near Marceline, Missouri, a typical small [Midwestern](https://www.britannica.com/place/Midwest) town, which is said to have furnished the inspiration and model for the Main Street, U.S.A., of Disneyland. There Walt began his schooling and first showed a taste and aptitude for [drawing](https://www.britannica.com/art/drawing-art) and painting with crayons and watercolors.

His restless father soon abandoned his efforts at farming and moved the family to [Kansas City](https://www.britannica.com/place/Kansas-City-Missouri) , Missouri, where he bought a morning newspaper route and compelled his young sons to assist him in delivering papers. Walt later said that many of the habits and compulsions of his adult life stemmed from the [disciplines](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/disciplines) and discomforts of helping his father with the paper route. In Kansas City the young Walt began to study cartooning with a correspondence school and later took classes at the Kansas City Art Institute and School of Design.

How Walt Disney became an American icon Learn more about the life, career, and legacy of Walt Disney. (more)

See all videos for this article

In 1917 the Disneys moved back to Chicago, and Walt entered McKinley High School, where he took photographs, made drawings for the school paper, and studied cartooning on the side, for he was hopeful of eventually achieving a job as a newspaper cartoonist. His progress was interrupted by [World War I](https://www.britannica.com/event/World-War-I) , in which he participated as an ambulance driver for the [American Red Cross](https://www.britannica.com/topic/American-Red-Cross) in France and Germany.

Returning to Kansas City in 1919, he found occasional employment as a draftsman and inker in commercial art studios, where he met [Ub Iwerks](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Ub-Iwerks) , a young artist whose talents contributed greatly to Walt’s early success.

Britannica Quiz Oscar-Worthy Movie Trivia

## First [animated](https://www.britannica.com/art/animation) cartoons

_Steamboat Willie_ , 1928 The animated _Steamboat Willie_ (1928) was the first Mickey Mouse film to include sound. (more)

See all videos for this article

Dissatisfied with their progress, Disney and Iwerks started a small studio of their own in 1922 and [acquired](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/acquired) a secondhand [movie camera](https://www.britannica.com/technology/motion-picture-camera) with which they made one and two-minute animated advertising films for distribution to local [movie](https://www.britannica.com/art/film) theaters. They also did a series of animated [cartoon](https://www.britannica.com/art/cartoon-pictorial-parody) sketches called _Laugh-O-grams_ and the pilot film for a series of seven-minute fairy tales that combined both live action and [animation](https://www.britannica.com/art/animation) , _Alice in Cartoonland_ . A [New York](https://www.britannica.com/place/New-York-state) film distributor cheated the young producers, and Disney was forced to file for bankruptcy in 1923. He moved to [California](https://www.britannica.com/place/California-state) to pursue a career as a cinematographer, but the surprise success of the first Alice film compelled Disney and his brother [Roy](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Roy-Disney) —a lifelong business partner—to reopen shop in Hollywood.

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/93/251993-050-384B2704/American-movie-producer-artist-and-animator-Walt-Disney.jpg)

[Walt Disney](https://cdn.britannica.com/93/251993-050-384B2704/American-movie-producer-artist-and-animator-Walt-Disney.jpg) Disney Company founder Walt Disney (center) talking with a pair of unidentified animators, California, c. 1950s. (more)

With Roy as business manager, Disney resumed the Alice series, persuading Iwerks to join him and assist with the drawing of the cartoons. They invented a character called [Oswald the Lucky Rabbit](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Oswald-the-Lucky-Rabbit) , contracted for distribution of the films at $1,500 each, and propitiously launched their small enterprise. In 1927, just before the [transition](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/transition) to sound in motion pictures, Disney and Iwerks experimented with a new character—a cheerful, energetic, and mischievous mouse called Mickey. They had planned two shorts, called _Plane Crazy_ and _Gallopin’ Gaucho_ , that were to introduce [Mickey Mouse](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Mickey-Mouse) when _The Jazz Singer_ , a motion picture with the popular singer [Al Jolson](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Al-Jolson) , brought the novelty of sound to the movies. Fully recognizing the possibilities for sound in animated-cartoon films, Disney quickly produced a third Mickey Mouse cartoon equipped with voices and music, entitled _[Steamboat Willie](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Steamboat-Willie)_ , and cast aside the other two soundless cartoon films. When it appeared in 1928, _Steamboat Willie_ was a sensation.

[SUBSCRIBE](https://premium.britannica.com/premium-membership/?utm_source=premium&utm_medium=inline-cta&utm_campaign=shorter-2026)

The following year Disney started a new series called [Silly Symphonies](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Silly-Symphonies) with a picture entitled _[The Skeleton Dance](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Skeleton-Dance)_ , in which a skeleton rises from the graveyard and does a [grotesque](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/grotesque) , clattering dance set to music based on classical themes. Original and briskly syncopated, the film ensured popular acclaim for the series, but, with costs mounting because of the more complicated drawing and technical work, Disney’s operation was continually in peril.

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/60/59660-050-F5B80706/Walt-Disney-use-motion-pictures-colour-sound.jpg)

[_The Three Little Pigs_](https://cdn.britannica.com/60/59660-050-F5B80706/Walt-Disney-use-motion-pictures-colour-sound.jpg) Walt Disney pioneered the use of sound and color in motion pictures with such cartoon shorts as _The Three Little Pigs_ (1933). (more)

The growing popularity of Mickey Mouse and his girlfriend, Minnie, however, attested to the public’s taste for the fantasy of little creatures with the speech, skills, and personality traits of human beings. (Disney himself provided the voice for Mickey until 1947.) This popularity led to the invention of other animal characters, such as Donald Duck and the dogs Pluto and Goofy. In 1933 Disney produced a short, _[The Three Little Pigs](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Three-Little-Pigs-cartoon-1933)_ , which arrived in the midst of the [Great Depression](https://www.britannica.com/event/Great-Depression) and took the country by storm. Its treatment of the [fairy tale](https://www.britannica.com/art/fairy-tale) of the little pig who works hard and builds his house of brick against the huffing and puffing of a threatening wolf suited the need for [fortitude](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/fortitude) in the face of economic disaster, and its song “Who’s Afraid of the Big Bad Wolf?”was a happy taunting of adversity. It was in this period of economic hard times in the early 1930s that Disney fully endeared himself and his cartoons to audiences all over the world, and his operation began making money in spite of the Depression.

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/70/24470-050-C0951667/Walt-Disney.jpg)

[Would you like a bedtime story, Donald?](https://cdn.britannica.com/70/24470-050-C0951667/Walt-Disney.jpg) Walt Disney pretends to read _Alice in Wonderland_ to a stuffed figure of Donald Duck, 1954. (more)

Disney had by that time gathered a staff of creative young people, who were headed by Iwerks. Color was introduced in the Academy Award-winning Silly Symphonies film _Flowers and Trees_ (1932), while other animal characters came and went in films such as _The Grasshopper and the Ants_ (1934) and _The Tortoise and the Hare_ (1935). Roy franchised tie-in sales with the cartoons of Mickey Mouse and Donald Duck—watches, [dolls](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/dolls) , shirts, and tops—and reaped more wealth for the company.
